title: Configure and Initialize a Repository
summary: Set a repository-local author identity and turn an existing project folder into a repository on main.

sketchBlocks.title: Configuring User Identity in Git
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
To start using Git effectively, it's crucial to configure your user identity. This involves setting your name and email address, which will be associated with your commits. This configuration can be done globally or locally within a specific repository. 

Worked example:

```bash
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
```

In this example, we set the user name and email for the current repository. This ensures that all commits made in this repository will be attributed to this identity. It's important to configure this correctly to maintain a clear commit history.

sketchBlocks.title: Turning a Folder into a Git Repository
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Initializing a Git repository is the first step in tracking your project's history. This process creates a `.git` directory in your project folder, which contains all the metadata and object database for your repository.

Worked example:

```bash
git init
```

This command initializes a new Git repository in the current directory. After running this command, your project folder becomes a Git repository, and you can start tracking changes. The initial branch is set to `main`, which is a common convention.

sketchBlocks.title: Understanding the Git Status Command
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Once your repository is initialized, it's important to understand the state of your working directory. The `git status` command provides a summary of changes in your working directory and staging area.

Worked example:

```bash
git status
```

This command shows which files are staged for the next commit, which are modified but unstaged, and which are untracked. It's a crucial tool for understanding what changes are ready to be committed and what still needs attention.

sketchBlocks.title: The Role of the .git Directory
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The `.git` directory is the heart of any Git repository. It contains all the metadata and object database for your project. Understanding its structure can help you appreciate how Git manages your project's history.

Worked example:

```bash
ls -a .git
```

This command lists the contents of the `.git` directory, revealing various subdirectories and files that store information about branches, configurations, and the commit history. While you should not manually edit these files, knowing their purpose is beneficial for advanced Git usage.

quizDraft.title: Purpose of Version Control
quizDraft.prompt: What is the primary purpose of version control systems like Git?
options: To track changes and manage project history
To provide a user interface for coding
quizDraft.hint: Think about how version control helps manage changes in projects.

help.concept: Version control systems track changes to files over time, allowing for collaboration and history tracking.
help.hint_1: Version control helps manage changes and track history in projects.
help.hint_2: It allows multiple people to work on a project simultaneously without losing track of changes.

quizDraft.title: Git Repository Initialization
quizDraft.prompt: What command is used to initialize a new Git repository?
quizDraft.hint: Consider the command that sets up a new repository in the current directory.

help.concept: Initializing a repository sets up the necessary structure for Git to track changes.
help.hint_1: The command creates a .git directory in the current folder.
help.hint_2: It is the first step in starting to track a project with Git.

quizDraft.title: Components of a Git Repository
quizDraft.prompt: Which of the following are components of a Git repository? Select all that apply.
quizDraft.hint: Think about the elements that make up the structure of a Git repository.

help.concept: A Git repository consists of several components that manage and track changes.
help.hint_1: Consider the parts that store metadata and track changes.
help.hint_2: These components include areas for staging, history, and configuration.

quizDraft.title: Order of Git Initialization Steps
quizDraft.prompt: Arrange the steps to initialize a Git repository in the correct order.
quizDraft.hint: Think about the sequence of actions needed to start tracking a project with Git.

help.concept: Initializing a repository involves setting up the environment and starting tracking.
help.hint_1: Consider the initial setup and configuration steps.
help.hint_2: The process begins with setting up the directory and configuring identity.

git config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

quizDraft.title: Explore the .git Directory
quizDraft.prompt:
Configure a repository-only Git identity in `project_delta` using the name `ZoeSkoul Learner` and email `learner@zoeskoul.local`.

First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter. Enter project_delta, run each local Git configuration command, and press Enter after each one.

quizDraft.starterCode:

quizDraft.instructions:
Configure a repository-only Git identity in `project_delta` using the name `ZoeSkoul Learner` and email `learner@zoeskoul.local`.

First type `bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh` and press Enter. Enter project_delta, run each local Git configuration command, and press Enter after each one.

starterFiles.content:
# First type: bash .zoeskoul/setup.sh
# Press Enter, then follow the task in the lesson prompt.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf -- "project_delta"
mkdir -p -- "project_delta"
git -C "project_delta" init -q -b main

solutionFiles.content:

cd project_delta
git config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"

#!/usr/bin/env bash

mkdir -p -- "project_delta"
git -C "project_delta" init -q -b main

hiddenShellCheck.script: test "$(git -C project_delta config --local user.name)" = "ZoeSkoul Learner" && test "$(git -C project_delta config --local user.email)" = "learner@zoeskoul.local"
